1:
The Chronicler crosses back over the Jordan and opens his muster of the northern tribes with Issachar, whose four sons — Tola, Puah, Jashub, and Shimron — head the roll. The reformed John Gill sets the list beside the older registers, noting that the same four names appear, with a small variation in two of them (there written Phuvah and Job, as in Gen. 46:13), in Num. 26:23, whence so many families of the tribe sprang. The historian, as throughout, is copying what he received, and Gill is content to mark the correspondence rather than press the difference of spelling.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the whole tribe through the patriarch's blessing. Jacob had compared Issachar to a strong ass couching between two burdens (Gen. 49:14), an industrious people who minded their country business closely and rejoiced in their tents (Deut. 33:18); and here, Henry says, they appear both numerous and valiant. They were numerous because they had many wives — so fruitful was their land that they feared no over-stocking of the pasture, and so ingenious the people that they found work for all hands; let no people complain of their numbers, he adds, provided they suffer none to be idle. They were valiant too, men of might and chief men, for those inured to labour and business were of all men the fittest to serve their country when there was occasion.

Henry reckons the several families, as numbered in the days of David, at above a hundred and forty-five thousand men fit for war, and pauses over the source of the count. Some think it was taken when Joab numbered the people at 2 Sam. 24; but Henry rather refers it to some other computation, perhaps made among themselves, since it is said that Joab's account was not entered in the chronicles of king David, that reckoning having offended God (1 Chron. 27:24). The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke stands back to survey the design, laying out the chapter as the genealogy of Issachar, then Benjamin, Naphtali, Manasseh, Ephraim, and Asher in turn — the settled tribes of the land gathered one by one into the sacred register.
2:
The eldest of Issachar's sons opens the count, and the witnesses turn at once to the numbers. The reformed John Gill notes only that Tola's posterity are reckoned here by name — Uzzi, Rephaiah, Jeriel, Jahmai, Jibsam, and Shemuel — the six heads of houses descended from him. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch observe that these six are met with nowhere else in the Old Testament; they were heads of their fathers'-houses of Tola, and the somewhat peculiar Hebrew, they judge, can only mean the fathers'-houses that are descended from Tola. The clause about their generations, they insist, belongs not to the word for valiant men but to what follows: registered according to their births, the males of these houses numbered twenty-two thousand six hundred in the days of David.

On the origin of that figure the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke and Keil and Delitzsch reason alike but hold it loosely. Keil and Delitzsch suppose the statement rests on the results of the census David had Joab take, recorded at 2 Sam. 24 and 1 Chron. 21. Clarke is more cautious: whether this was the number returned by Joab and his assistants, when they made that census with which God was so displeased, he says plainly, we know not. He marks a real curiosity of the chapter — that here alone we read the sum of three tribes, Benjamin, Issachar, and Asher, under the reign of David, while of the other tribes no such reckoning is given, probably because the author found no public registers in which their numbers were recorded.

So a mild disagreement runs beneath the verse. Where Keil and Delitzsch confidently trace the muster to Joab's numbering, Clarke leaves the question open, and Henry, at the head of the chapter, had preferred to think of some separate computation, precisely because Joab's ill-fated census was said never to have been entered in the royal chronicles. What the witnesses share is the sense that these dry figures are not idle: the twenty-two thousand of Tola's house are the fighting strength of one clan of one tribe, a fragment of the great host that Issachar, industrious and fruitful, could bring into the field, and a token of how faithfully the Chronicler preserved even the military reckonings of the settled tribes.
3:
From Uzzi, the firstborn of Tola, the line runs on to Izrahiah and his sons, and the witnesses attend both to the descent and to a small puzzle in the counting. The reformed John Gill takes the sons of Uzzi to include his posterity through Izrahiah. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch trace the branch precisely: from Uzzi comes Izrahiah, and from Izrahiah five men — Michael, Obadiah, Joel, and Ishiah — all heads of groups of related households, and to them, besides, bands of the war-host numbering thirty-six thousand, for these chiefs had many wives and sons. Keil and Delitzsch pause to correct Bertheau, who inferred from Izrahiah being Tola's great-grandson that these verses must belong to a time later than David; that is an erroneous inference, they answer, for Tola's own sons did not live in David's day at all, yet their descendants might well have multiplied to the number given by David's time.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fastens on the arithmetic. The text says the sons of Izrahiah were five, yet names only four; the Syriac and Arabic versions accordingly read four. If five be the true reading, Clarke resolves, then Izrahiah must be reckoned along with his four sons, the father counted in with the children — a simple expedient that keeps the received number without emending the text.

The witnesses thus make of a bare list a small lesson in how these registers are to be read. The five heads and their thirty-six thousand are not a fresh generation sprung up after David but the swelling of an old stock, counted at the height of its strength; and the discrepancy of five against four is met, as such things are met throughout the chapter, either by including the head with his sons or by the sober notice of a variant reading. Keil and Delitzsch's care to distinguish the date of the men from the date of their multiplication, and Clarke's patience with the counting, alike guard the reader against pressing an abridged genealogy as though it were a modern census, and let the fruitfulness of Issachar — many wives, many sons, whole bands for the war — stand out as the thing the numbers were kept to show.
4:
The verse gathers the sons of Izrahiah into a military reckoning, and the witnesses read it as the visible fruit of the tribe's fertility. The reformed John Gill glosses the phrase simply: with them, by their generations, after the house of their fathers, were bands of soldiers for war — companies of men of military courage and skill, who could and did take the field upon occasion. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch supply the figure and its cause: these bands numbered thirty-six thousand men, and the text itself explains the largeness of the muster, for they had many wives and sons. The register, they note, then passes to the brethren of the whole tribe, before turning at the sixth verse to Benjamin.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, surveying the tribe at the head of the chapter, had already drawn out the moral hidden in these numbers. It was the mark of Issachar that they were at once fruitful and industrious: so productive was their land that they saw no danger of over-stocking the pasture though their households were large, and so ingenious the people that they could find work for every hand. Their many wives and many sons, far from being a burden, became the strength of the tribe, thirty-six thousand fighting men out of a single branch; and Henry's rule presses upon the verse — let no people complain of their numbers, provided they suffer none to be idle.

So the witnesses take this dry entry as more than a tally of troops. The bands of soldiers are the grown sons of fruitful houses, the increase God had given to a tribe that laboured and did not sit idle; and the historian, who elsewhere gives only names, here lets the reason stand in the open — "for they had many wives and sons" — so that the reader may see the muster of Issachar as the plain arithmetic of blessing. What Jacob had foreseen in the strong ass couching between its burdens, and what the fruitful fields of the tribe had made possible, is here counted out in fighting men: a people that worked and multiplied, and could arm thirty-six thousand from one stem of one of its clans.
5:
The count of Issachar closes with the brethren of all its families gathered into one great sum. The reformed John Gill reads the brethren as the men of the other houses of the tribe — those of Puah, Jashub, and Shimron — set beside the descendants of Tola already numbered. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch give the total plainly: reckoned in all by their genealogies, the valiant men of might throughout the families of Issachar came to fourscore and seven thousand.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, weighing the whole tribe, adds the several branches together and marvels at the strength of it — above a hundred and forty-five thousand men fit for war, drawn from a people the patriarch had likened to a labouring beast of burden. It was, he says, a valiant tribe, men of might and chief men, for those inured to labour and business proved of all men the fittest to serve their country when there was occasion; the industry that filled their fields also filled their armies. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke marks again the singularity of the record, that here the fighting strength of Issachar is set down under David as it is for only two other tribes, Benjamin and Asher, the rest being passed over for want of registers.

The witnesses converge on the meaning the number carries. Eighty-seven thousand valiant men from one tribe is no idle boast but the token of a promise kept: the fruitful, hard-working sons of Issachar had grown, under the blessing pronounced over their father, into a great host. Gill's care to identify the brethren with the families of Puah and the rest, and Keil and Delitzsch's sober total, and Henry's reflection on labour and might, all serve one point — that the God who had multiplied this tribe in its tents and fields had made it strong for war as well, so that the dry sum at the end of the muster is in truth a memorial of divine faithfulness to the seed of Jacob. Clarke's observation that Issachar is one of only three tribes whose Davidic muster survives lends the figure a further poignancy, for where the other tribes' registers had perished, this one was kept; and Henry's reflection on labour and might hovers over the whole, reminding the reader that the eighty-seven thousand were not idlers but the hardy sons of a working people. With that the historian has done with Issachar, and turns to number the sons of Benjamin.
6:
With Benjamin the historian's method grows plainly visible, for the sons here counted do not agree in number with the older records, and the witnesses face the difficulty head on. The reformed John Gill notes that Benjamin had ten sons, yet three only — Bela, Becher, and Jediael — are named first, and reckons the last of these the same as the Ashbel of the earlier lists. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke lays out the tangle in full: Gen. 46:21 reckons ten sons of Benjamin, Num. 26:38 only five, with Ard and Naaman there made grandsons through Bela, and the next chapter names five sons again with a further crowd of grandsons. Hence, Clarke concludes, grandsons are often called sons, and the two are constantly confounded in the genealogical tables; the rabbins say that Ezra, who wrote this book, did not himself know whether some were sons or grandsons, and that the tables he copied were often defective. To attempt to reconcile such discrepancies, Clarke judges, would be a task as endless as it would be useless.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the philology. Becher, pointed with different vowels, would mean "firstborn," so that the original reading in the old record may have been "Bela his firstborn," as at 1 Chron. 8:1; and Jediael, "friend of God," he takes to be a substitute for Ashbel, that is Eshbaal, "man of Baal" — a pious exchange like Eliada for Beeliada at 1 Chron. 3:8. Ashbel, he notes, stands second among Benjamin's sons in one list and third in another.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley offers the plainest reckoning of why three appear where ten are known: they were ten, he grants, and five of them are named in the following chapter, but here only three are mentioned, either because these were the most eminent, or because the other families were by the Chronicler's day extinct. The witnesses do not resolve every difference, and Clarke would not have them try; they leave Benjamin's three sons as the heads the historian chose to follow, transcribed faithfully from sources that did not always agree. The convergence of Gill and Ellicott upon the point that Jediael answers to Ashbel, and the frank confession of Clarke and the rabbins that Ezra himself may not have known which names were sons and which grandsons, together set the tone for the whole account of Benjamin: a genealogy honestly preserved in its variety, not smoothed into a spurious agreement, and the more trustworthy for the roughness the Chronicler left in it.
7:
The sons of Bela are given as five — Ezbon, Uzzi, Uzziel, Jerimoth, and Iri — with a muster of twenty-two thousand and thirty-four, and the witnesses agree that these are not sons in the strict sense. The reformed John Gill thinks them rather grandsons of Bela, since the names differ from those of the parallel list. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch put it exactly: the five called sons of Bela are not sons proper but more distant descendants who, when this register was drawn up, were heads of the five groups of related households of the race of Bela; and they pause over the grammar of the phrase for valiant heroes, which has taken a plural form as though from a single compounded word, the two terms so often bound together that their relation was obscured.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens the historical point. The names here are wholly different from those given as Bela's sons at 1 Chron. 8:3, and the reason, he judges, is that the present names represent the chieftains and clans of Bela as they stood at a given epoch, the time of David's census, while the other list belongs to another period. Here, as elsewhere, it is evident that the Chronicler has faithfully followed, or rather transcribed, his sources without a thought of harmonizing their apparent inconsistencies. The heads named are the chieftains of Bela's father-houses, and the figure of twenty-two thousand and thirty-four is the fighting strength of the Belaites, who are here identified with their heads.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the verse the same way, taking the five to be each the head of a family to which he belonged, and, comparing the next chapter, judges that these were not the immediate sons of Bela but his grandchildren, descended each from a several father. So the witnesses stand together: whether one calls them grandsons or clan-heads, the list is a census of Bela's fighting men gathered under the chieftains of his day, and the differences from the older record are no contradiction but the natural mark of registers taken at different times and faithfully preserved.
8:
The line of Becher spreads into nine names, and the witnesses find in several of them the trace of towns as well as men. The reformed John Gill marks only that these are the sons of Becher, another son of Benjamin. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch observe that two of the nine — Anathoth and Alameth — occur elsewhere as names of cities, and they accept the supposition that the cities took their names from the heads of the families which inhabited them. The register that follows, they add, is reckoned according to the generations of the heads of these fathers'-houses.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott develops the point at length. The nine Benjamite houses here enumerated, recorded nowhere else, might well have been known as "sons of the firstborn," Becher's name carrying that sense; and among them the name Elioenai is one that recurs frequently through the Chronicles. Anathoth and Alameth, he notes, were Levitical towns in Benjamin, named together at 1 Chron. 6:60 — Anathoth the later home of the prophet Jeremiah (Jer. 1:1) — and Jerimoth too, which stood among the sons of Bela in the previous verse, looks like another local name. The clans, Ellicott concludes, may well have borne the names of their seats, so that this stretch of the genealogy is at once a roll of chieftains and a map of the towns of Benjamin.

The witnesses thus turn a bare list into a small geography of the tribe. Where the modern eye sees only a string of unfamiliar names, Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott see the founders of households whose memory lingered in the very places they settled, Anathoth and Alameth keeping their names long after the men were gone. It is a further instance of the principle that governs the whole chapter: these registers count not merely individuals but the fathers-houses and their seats, the clans of Becher rooted in the soil of Benjamin, each chief giving his name to a town and each town preserving the name of its chief. Ellicott's care to note how often Elioenai and Jerimoth recur elsewhere in the Chronicles only strengthens the point, that the same family-names run through many places and many generations, so that a reader who would trace them must attend to the seats as closely as to the men. The nine houses of Becher, unrecorded in any other book, are thus set down here as the abiding memorial of a branch of Benjamin whose sons filled a whole cluster of Benjamite towns.
9:
The house of Becher is now summed in its fighting strength, and the witnesses weigh both the figure and the manner of reckoning it. The reformed John Gill takes the number to be that of the posterity of Becher's sons — the grown descendants of the nine houses just named. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch give it as the register demands: reckoned by their genealogies, after their generations, the heads of the fathers'-houses, mighty men of valour, amounted to twenty thousand and two hundred.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the clause carefully — their census, according to their birth-rolls, heads of their clans, valiant warriors, was twenty thousand two hundred — and explains that this means the total number of the warriors of Becher, chiefs together with their clans. The census, he stresses, is that of the chiefs who are here regarded as one with their clans, the head and the household counted as a single body. And he notes the wider question such lists raise: others assume that the names in these registers are merely those of supposed founders of the clans, eponyms like Hellen, Ion, and Dorus among the Greeks, or Italus, Latinus, Romulus, and Remus among the Romans — legendary ancestors whose names label a people rather than record a man.

The witnesses leave the tension unforced. Whether one takes the heads to be real chieftains numbered with their followers, as Ellicott mainly does, or bare eponyms who lend their names to the clans, the sense of the verse is the same: twenty thousand two hundred armed men stood under the banner of Becher's house. Gill's plain reading of it as the posterity of Becher's sons, and Ellicott's identification of the chiefs with their clans, meet in the one truth the number is kept to preserve — the martial strength of a single branch of Benjamin, gathered under its family heads and set down in the sacred roll so that the tribe's contribution to the host of Israel might not be lost. The Chronicler counts, as always, not for curiosity but to show a people grown strong under the promise.
10:
The third of Benjamin's sons yields the richest cluster of names, and Ellicott mines it for the mixed origins of the tribe. The reformed John Gill notes simply that the sons of Jediael are traced through Bilhan and his posterity — Jeush, Benjamin, Ehud, Chenaanah, Zethan, Tharshish, and Ahishahar. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott finds in almost every name a story. Bilhan is a name borne also by a son of Seir the Horite at 1 Chron. 1:42, and Ellicott wonders whether it marks an Edomite element absorbed into Benjamin, comparing the way foreign stock was taken up elsewhere in these registers. Jeush is the name of a son of Esau at 1 Chron. 1:35; and it is curious, he remarks, that a Benjamite clan should bear the tribal name Benjamin itself.

Ellicott presses on through the list. Ehud is a namesake of the judge who slew Eglon the Moabite oppressor at Judg. 3:15, though that Ehud sprang from Gera, a division of Bela, and so is not this man. Chenaanah, meaning "Canaanitess," is perhaps a Canaanite house that had amalgamated with the sons of Jediael; Tharshish elsewhere names the famous Phoenician colony in Spain, here serving as a man's name; and Ahishahar, "brother of dawn," he sets beside other names of the same cast, suggesting a comparison with the common Arab designation "sons of the east."

The witnesses thus read the roll of Jediael as a record of Benjamin's incorporations. Where Gill is content to name the descendants, Ellicott hears in Bilhan, Jeush, Chenaanah, and the rest the echoes of Edom, Esau, and Canaan, peoples drawn into the tribe and given a place in its households; even the appearance of the name Benjamin among the clans, and of an Ehud who is not the judge, warns the reader that these names travel between tribes and persons. The chapter's larger lesson holds here too: the Chronicler transcribes what he found, mixed origins and all, and the genealogy of Benjamin proves to be a weaving of many strands into one covenant tribe. Even Ahishahar, "brother of dawn," Ellicott sets beside the eastern manner of naming, as though the household looked toward the sunrise; and the whole roll of Jediael, eight names strong, stands as evidence that the small tribe of Benjamin had gathered into itself the remnants of older peoples and made them its own, so that its muster in the next verse counts not one pure stock but many, knit together under the sons of Jacob's youngest.
11:
The muster of Jediael's house closes the count of Benjamin, and here the witnesses divide over the sum. The reformed John Gill gives the seventeen thousand two hundred soldiers of Jediael, fit to go out for war, and then adds the branches together to make of the tribe of Benjamin, besides what follows, fifty-nine thousand four hundred and thirty; and he supposes that if these were numbered by Joab, his account was never given in. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch confirm the seventeen thousand two hundred as the men of Jediael, mighty men of valour ready for battle, and note that the next verse, with its Shuppim and Huppim, is by contrast scarcely intelligible.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reckons the tribe differently. Rendering the heads as one with their warriors, he makes the sum of the armed men of Benjamin fifty-four thousand four hundred and thirty-four, and sets it against the Mosaic census, where the tribe stood at forty-five thousand six hundred (Num. 26:41). An increase of barely fourteen thousand across at least three centuries may seem too small, he grants; but the tribe had been well-nigh exterminated in the vengeance Israel took for the crime of Gibeah, when it was cut down to a remnant, as the old story tells at Judg. 20:47.

So a real discrepancy stands in the open: Gill's total of fifty-nine thousand four hundred and thirty against Ellicott's fifty-four thousand four hundred and thirty-four, the difference turning on how the overlapping branches are added. Neither witness pretends the figures are simple, and both feel the strangeness of a tribe grown so little since Moses. Yet Ellicott's appeal to the near-destruction at Gibeah turns even the smallness of the number into testimony: Benjamin, all but wiped out for its sin, had been brought back from the edge of extinction to field its fifty thousand again, a remnant multiplied, so that the modest muster is itself a quiet record of judgment survived and mercy shown. Gill, for his part, does not press his higher total as a proof against Joab but rather leaves it as a sum the royal chronicles never received, of a piece with his reading of the earlier tribes; and Keil and Delitzsch, content to give the figure of Jediael's house and mark the seventeen thousand two hundred ready for war, pass on without adjudicating the difference. The witnesses thus close the account of Benjamin with its numbers unresolved but its meaning plain — a tribe of mighty men of valour, few by the measure of Moses, and precious for having been so nearly lost.
12:
The verse that ends the account of Benjamin is, by common confession, the hardest in the chapter, and the witnesses labour over both its names and its silence about Dan. The reformed John Gill takes Shuppim and Huppim to be the children of Ir, that is Iri of the seventh verse, so that they are not sons of Benjamin as they seem but his great-grandchildren, the same as the Shupham and Hupham from whom families sprang; the Targum, he adds, calls them the inhabitants of a city. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch frankly call the verse unintelligible to us, judging Muppim a copyist's error for Shupham, and Ir identical with the Iri of the seventh verse, the odd forms having arisen from an improper comparison with the fifteenth verse.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott gives the fullest treatment and raises the great question. Shuppim and Huppim he takes for further Benjamite clans thrown in at the close, sons of Ir, that is of Iri son of Bela; there is no more contradiction, he says, in calling the same man son of Ir, son of Bela, and son of Benjamin than in tracing a man to David, Judah, and Abraham at once. But the last clause, "Hushim, the sons of Aher," draws his closest attention. Aher means "another," and since Gen. 46:23 gives "the sons of Dan, Hushim," in just this position between Benjamin and Naphtali, some have read the phrase as a veiled reference to the tribe of Dan, whose name is otherwise omitted from the chapter. The coincidence is remarkable, Ellicott owns; yet he weighs against it that Num. 26:42 calls Dan's offspring Shuham, not Hushim, that Zebulun is dropped here as well as Dan, and that the Chronicler names Dan freely at 1 Chron. 2:2. He concludes that the whole verse is simply an appendix to the genealogy of Benjamin.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, however, keeps the other reading alive, noting that the sons of Dan were Hushim, and that some render Aher appellatively, "the sons of another" — a hint, he thinks, of the tribe whose idolatry at Laish and whose golden calf under Jeroboam made it fit to be passed over in silence, as it is likewise omitted at Rev. 7. So the debate stands unresolved between them.
13:
Naphtali is dispatched in a single verse, and the witnesses note how little the historian keeps of it. The reformed John Gill records the four sons — Jahziel, Guni, Jezer, and Shallum — observing only that Shallum is the Shillem of the older lists. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch remark that here only the sons of Naphtali are named, the families descended from them being wholly passed over, and that the names answer to those of Gen. 46:24 and Num. 26:48, save that there the first is written Jahzeel and the last Shallem rather than Shallum.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott confirms the correspondence and guards against a false inference. The sons of Naphtali are called the sons of Bilhah, Jacob's handmaid; and since Dan too was Bilhah's son (Gen. 46:25), some might look here for a trace of the missing tribe — but that, Ellicott insists, is not intended, the phrase marking only Naphtali's descent from the handmaid. In both the older record and the present text, he adds, grandsons are freely reckoned among the sons.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry feels the poverty of the notice and offers a reason for it. Of Naphtali the first fathers only are named, the very same that stand in Gen. 46:24, and none of their descendants; perhaps, he suggests, because the genealogies of the tribe had been lost by the Chronicler's day. The witnesses do not strain to fill the gap. They let Naphtali pass with its four ancestral names, content to mark the agreement with the law and to own, with Henry, that where the records had perished the historian could set down no more. Keil and Delitzsch underline how spare the entry is beside the fuller lines around it: the sons are named, but not one of the families that sprang from them, as though the register here had run dry. Ellicott's reminder that Naphtali is traced through Bilhah, the handmaid whose sons were Dan and Naphtali, keeps the tribe in its proper place among the children of Jacob, even while it guards against making the mention of Bilhah a back-door to the omitted Dan. The single verse becomes a quiet witness to the imperfect state of the sources the Chronicler worked from, and to his honesty in giving only what he had — the bare heads of a tribe whose after-generations were beyond his recovery, set down that Naphtali might not be wholly absent from the roll of the tribes.
14:
With Manasseh the register grows obscure, and the witnesses divide at once over who bore Asriel. The reformed John Gill reads "Ashriel, whom she bare" of Manasseh's wife, distinguished from the concubine of the next clause, though he notes that the Targum joins the clauses so that the Aramitess concubine is the mother. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch treat the whole passage as dealing with the families of Manasseh that were settled west of Jordan, of the six known families naming only two or three here; Asriel, they observe, belonged according to the law to the sons of Gilead, Manasseh's grandson through Machir (Gen. 50:23), and they own the statements of these two verses to be very obscure.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the verse against the fuller genealogy of the law: "the sons of Manasseh, Asriel, whom his Aramean concubine bare — she bare Machir, father of Gilead." The line runs, as Num. 27:1 and Josh. 17:3 show, from Manasseh through Machir and Gilead down to Zelophehad, who had five daughters but no sons; and since the older record at Num. 26:29 declares all these names to be clans, and makes Asriel a great-grandson of Manasseh, the parenthesis about Machir must be meant to warn the reader that Asriel sprang from the Aramean concubine only mediately, through descent from Machir.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads with Gill: "she" is his wife, the concubine being set in opposition to her. So the witnesses genuinely divide — Gill and Wesley making a wife of Manasseh the mother of Asriel and the concubine a separate matter, Ellicott and the Targum making the Aramean concubine the fountain of the whole line through Machir. The disagreement is not idle, for it touches how much foreign blood stood at the head of West Manasseh; but all agree that the verse, dark as it is, means to trace this half-tribe back through Machir and Gilead to the patriarch, and to mark, in the Aramitess, an early mingling of Israel with the nations even before the bondage in Egypt was full-grown. Keil and Delitzsch's reminder that of the six Manassite families only two or three are treated here, and Ellicott's careful placing of Asriel three generations down from Manasseh, together caution the reader that the historian is not opening the whole tree of the tribe but tracing selected clans; the very presence of a Syrian concubine at the head of the line becomes, on either reading, a token that the covenant people were never a pure and unmixed stock, but a house into which the nations had married from the first.
15:
The obscurity deepens at the fifteenth verse, and the witnesses confess it while trying to make sense of Machir's marriages. The reformed John Gill reads that Machir took to wife the sister of Huppim and Shuppim, whose name was Maachah, so that the Manassite chief married into the tribe of Benjamin. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch object that the Hebrew literally says Machir "took a wife to Huppim and Shuppim," which cannot be reconciled with the next verse, where Maachah is plainly Machir's own wife; from the words as they stand, they say, no meaning that suits the context can be drawn, and they can only suppose that by Huppim and Shuppim some of Machir's sons are meant, to whom he gave wives.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott agrees the received rendering is impossible and offers his own: "now Machir took a wife of Huppim and of Shuppim," the two Benjamite clans of the twelfth verse, the name of the first wife being Maachah and the name of the second having dropped from the text. He resists the temptation to make Zelophehad the second wife who bore only daughters, since Num. 26:33 and Josh. 17:3 make Zelophehad a man; a few words, he judges, have fallen out that once gave the second wife's name and Zelophehad's descent from her. The whole passage, he concludes, seems to assert both an Aramean and a Benjamite element in the population of Western Manasseh.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley cuts through more simply, taking "the second" to be the second son or grandson of Machir, namely Zelophehad, who had daughters only. The witnesses thus leave the verse where they found it — half in shadow — but not without gain. Whether Huppim and Shuppim be Machir's sons or the Benjamite houses into which he married, and whether a wife's name has dropped away or not, the drift is clear: the chieftain of Gilead knit his house to Benjamin by marriage, so that the blood of two tribes met in West Manasseh, and the daughters of Zelophehad, whose famous suit for inheritance the law records, stand already in view at the edge of the register.
16:
The line of Machir is carried forward through Maachah's children, and the witnesses read the verse as the resumption of a genealogy the previous confusion had interrupted. The reformed John Gill notes plainly that Maachah, the wife of Machir, bore him two sons, Peresh and Sheresh. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the descent on: Peresh and his brother Sheresh, and the sons of Peresh, Ulam and Rakem; and they gather the whole into the summary that these were the sons of Gilead, the son of Machir, the son of Manasseh, before the register turns aside to a further branch sprung from Machir's sister.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds what little more can be said. Peresh and Sheresh, he observes, occur nowhere else in Scripture; and Ulam and Rakem were probably the sons of the elder brother Peresh, whose line would naturally be continued, as usual, rather than the younger's. The commentators do not pretend to more, for the names are found in no other passage and yield no story beyond the bare descent. Keil and Delitzsch, gathering Peresh and Sheresh, Ulam and Rakem into the summary of "the sons of Gilead," treat the little branch as one more household of the half-tribe whose descent the register is bent on preserving; and Ellicott's guess that Ulam and Rakem belonged to the elder Peresh rests on the common rule that it is the firstborn's line which is carried forward, the younger brother's issue being noticed and then left.

Yet the witnesses feel the quiet significance of the entry after the darkness of the verses before. Where the marriages of Machir had grown almost impenetrable, here the genealogy steadies itself and resumes its plain work: Maachah bears her sons, the sons beget their sons, and the house of Gilead is traced generation by generation. The very ordinariness of the notice, Peresh and Sheresh and their children, is a kind of relief, the register recovering its footing and moving on. And in gathering these into "the sons of Gilead, the son of Machir, the son of Manasseh," the historian keeps his true aim in view — not the tangle of who married whom, but the unbroken descent of the half-tribe from the patriarch, each generation set down that Manasseh's place among the tribes of Israel might stand recorded and sure.
17:
A single son of Ulam is named, and the witnesses make of Bedan both a puzzle of identity and a knot of grammar. The reformed John Gill notes only the bare descent, the sons of Ulam being Bedan. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott takes Bedan to mean "ben Dan," the Danite, and recalls that a Bedan appears at 1 Sam. 12:11 as a judge set between Jerubbaal and Jephthah; but here, he insists, a clan is meant, not a person. The closing words — "these were the sons of Gilead, the son of Machir, the son of Manasseh" — seem to him to gather up a series of names that has dropped out of the text, names that may be recovered from Num. 26:30 and following, where Abiezer and Shemidah stand among the families of Manasseh.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, at the head of the chapter, had lingered on this same Bedan. He is perhaps the very man reckoned among Israel's deliverers at 1 Sam. 12:11, Henry suggests; and since Jair, who judged Israel, was of Manasseh (Judg. 10:3), it may be that Jair was the deliverer there intended under the name Bedan — a Manassite hero whose lineage this verse preserves.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the summarizing clause otherwise, taking "these" to refer not to Bedan and his immediate kin but back to Ashriel and Zelophehad, named earlier, the relative pointing to the remoter antecedent, as is frequent in Hebrew. So the witnesses circle the verse from several sides — Ellicott hearing in Bedan a clan and suspecting a gap in the text, Henry hoping to find in him a forgotten judge of Manasseh, Wesley disputing the reference of the closing words — yet all treat the short notice with the same care they give the fuller passages. Even a lone name and a summary clause, they show, may hide a deliverer of Israel, or a lost line of the register, and are not to be passed over as though the Chronicler had written them in vain. That Ellicott and Wesley should read the very same closing words so differently — the one referring them to the sons of Gilead and suspecting a gap, the other carrying them back to Ashriel and Zelophehad — shows how tightly compressed the passage is, and how much the interpreter must supply. Yet through all their differences the witnesses hold one thing fast: the house of Manasseh is here being traced with care through Machir and Gilead and Ulam, that not even its obscurer branches should drop out of the memory of Israel.
18:
The register turns aside to a woman, Gilead's sister Hammoleketh, and the witnesses attend both to her name and to the sons she bore. The reformed John Gill notes that Hammoleketh was the sister of Gilead, and that her name has been variously understood: the Targum renders it "that reigned," and Kimchi takes it to mean she reigned over some part of Gilead, while the Vulgate translates it as a title, "a queen bare Ishod, and Abiezer, and Mahalah." Of these sons Gill marks that Abiezer is the same as the Jeezer from whom a family sprang, and that of this family was Gideon.

The grammatical-historical witnesses fill out the descent. Keil and Delitzsch make Hammoleketh a third branch of Gilead's house, the sons of Machir's sister; her son Ishod, "man of splendour," is nowhere else named, and her Abiezer they distinguish from the head of the Manassite family at Josh. 17:2, who is called Jeezer at Num. 26:30 — a different man, they judge, since that Jeezer's family holds first place among the families of Manasseh, ill suiting the son of Machir's sister here. Charles Ellicott takes Hammoleketh, "the queen" of the Vulgate, to be a half-sister and consort of Gilead, and glosses Ish-hod as "man of majesty."

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads with care, noting that "his sister" means Gilead's sister, and that at "Mahalah" the reader must understand also "and Shemida" out of the following verse, the list running on past the verse-division. The witnesses thus rescue Hammoleketh from being a mere name, seeing in her a woman of rank — a queen, or one who reigned in Gilead — and mother of a line that would run down to Gideon the deliverer. It is a rare thing in these registers for a sister to be counted and her children named; the historian keeps her because through her, as through the wives and daughters elsewhere honoured, the tribe of Manasseh branched and was built up, and because from her house would come a judge to save Israel. Keil and Delitzsch's patience in separating this Abiezer from the elder Jeezer of the law, refusing to fuse two men because they share a name and a family, is of a piece with the whole chapter's discipline; and Gill's and Wesley's readings together let the verse run on unbroken into the next, "and Mahalah," and Shemida, so that Hammoleketh's children and Shemida's are gathered as branches of one Gileadite stock. The queen of Gilead, whatever her title meant, thus takes her place among the mothers through whom the promise to Manasseh was carried forward.
19:
The last of the Manassite branches is given in the sons of Shemida, and the witnesses are chiefly concerned to keep its names distinct from others that sound like them. The reformed John Gill takes Shemida for another of the family sprung from Gilead's line, from whom came the family of the Shemidaites known to the law at Num. 26:32. His four sons — Ahian, Shechem, Likhi, and Aniam — Gill sets down without further remark.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, who count Shemida the founder of the fourth family of the Manassites, warn that these four sons are nowhere else referred to, and are not to be confused with their near-homonyms. The Shechem of this verse must be distinguished from the Shechem who founded a Manassite family at Num. 26:31 and Josh. 17:2; there is no more reason to identify Likhi with the Helek of Num. 26:30 than to connect Aniam with Noam, the daughter of Zelophehad. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott agrees that the name Shechem points to the western half of Manasseh, as at Josh. 17:2, and that Ahian, Likhi, and Aniam are not mentioned elsewhere.

The witnesses' caution is itself the lesson of the verse. In a register so crowded with repeated names, where a Shechem or a Likhi may sound like a chief already met, the temptation is to collapse distinct men into one and to force the abridged list into a false neatness. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott resist it, insisting that these sons of Shemida are their own men, founders of their own households within West Manasseh, however little else is known of them. So the account of the half-tribe closes as it began, in obscurity honestly owned; the historian has traced Manasseh through Machir and Gilead and their branching houses down to Shemida's sons, and though many of the names yield no story, each is kept in its place, that the tribe settled on the west of Jordan might have its due record beside its brethren. It is worth marking how the same names circle back through these lists — a Shechem among the sons of Shemida and a Shechem among the founders of Manasseh, a Likhi that echoes Helek, an Aniam that chimes with Noam — and how firmly Keil and Delitzsch keep them apart. The lesson the witnesses draw is not learned pedantry but reverence for the text: the Chronicler wrote what he received, and the reader honours the record best by receiving each name as its own, neither forcing the abridged clans into a false harmony nor dissolving distinct households into one for the mere accident of a shared name.
20:
With Ephraim the genealogy suddenly opens into narrative, and the witnesses feel the change. The reformed John Gill traces the plain descent through five generations — Shuthelah, Bered, Tahath, Eladah, and a second Tahath — the line of Joseph's younger son carried down name by name. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch follow the descendants of Shuthelah, founder of one of the chief Ephraimite families, through six generations to a later Shuthelah, and take the names Ezer and Elead that follow to be heads of a family running parallel to his, whom "the men of Gath that were born in the land" slew. They reject the notion of Ewald and Bertheau that these native men of Gath were the Avvites of Deut. 2:23, finding no sufficient ground for it.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes the whole passage a meditation on providence crossing promise. Great things were read of Ephraim at its maturity, but here we have the disasters of its infancy, while the tribe was yet in Egypt and Ephraim himself, it seems, still alive — though the reckoning of seven generations makes Henry wonder whether it was another Ephraim, or whether the slain were the immediate sons of Joseph's son. Jacob had foretold that the seed of Ephraim should become a multitude of nations (Gen. 48:19), and yet the plant is here nipped in the bud. God's providences, Henry reflects, often seem to contradict his promises; but when they do so they really magnify the promise, and make the performance of it, notwithstanding all, so much the more illustrious.

The witnesses thus turn the opening of Ephraim's line into something more than a list. Where the other tribes were counted in bare names and numbers, here the register pauses over a wound — sons cut off, a family broken by the men of Gath — and the commentators read it against the great blessing spoken over Ephraim's head. Keil and Delitzsch labour to identify the slain and their slayers; Henry lifts his eyes to the promise that seemed, for a moment, to fail. Together they prepare the reader for the sorrow and the recovery that the next verses unfold, and for the truth that runs beneath the whole chapter: that the purposes of God for a tribe are not overthrown even when its children fall.
21:
The blow that fell on Ephraim is now told, and the witnesses gather around the slaughter and its cause. The reformed John Gill takes Zabad to be not the son of the Tahath just named but a second son of Ephraim himself. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the verse with the one before it: Zabad, Shuthelah, Ezer, and Elead were slain by the men of Gath that were born in the land, because they came down to take away their cattle — a raid on the herds that ended in blood, and left Ephraim to mourn.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry weighs who the aggressors were, and finds the question genuinely open. Some make the men of Gath the aggressors, coming down into the land of Goshen to drive away the Ephraimites' cattle and slaying the owners who defended them; others think the Ephraimites made the descent upon Gath, presuming the time was come to seize their inheritance, and paid dearly for their rashness — for those who will not wait God's time cannot expect his blessing. Henry himself inclines to the first, reading the words as "the men of Gath slew them, for they came down to take away their cattle," since the Israelites in Egypt were shepherds rather than soldiers, unlikely to venture their lives for a neighbour's herds. On the identity of the slain he follows Dr. Lightfoot, who takes them to be Zabad the son of Ephraim and his grandchildren Shuthelah, Ezer, and Elead.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reports the Targum's elaborate tale, which turns the disaster into a lesson in mistimed faith: the sons of Ephraim, it says, reckoned the appointed period from the covenant made with Abraham between the pieces, and erred, going out of Egypt thirty years before the time, so that two hundred thousand Ephraimite warriors were cut down by the men of Gath. The witnesses thus leave a real debate over who struck first — a raid suffered or a rebellion punished — while agreeing that the promise of Gen. 15 was not to be forced, and that the men of Gath, whoever provoked the quarrel, were the instruments of Ephraim's grief.
22:
The verse dwells on the father's grief, and the witnesses read it as a window into both the history and the heart. The reformed John Gill fixes the plain fact against those who would multiply Ephraims: the patriarch mourned many days for the loss of his son and grandchildren, for the deed was done while the Israelites were yet in Egypt and Ephraim himself still alive; there is no need, he insists, to suppose another Ephraim different from the son of Joseph.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry pours out the pathos of it. Nothing, he says, brings the aged to their grave with more sorrow than to follow to the grave the young that descend from them, and that in blood; it is often the burden of those who live to be old that they see go before them the very ones of whom they had said, These shall comfort us. Yet Henry finds comfort in the brethren who came to mourn with him: it was a brotherly and friendly office to visit the bereaved patriarch, to express their sympathy and to suggest what might support and quiet him under so sad a providence. Probably, he thinks, they reminded him of the promise of increase with which Jacob had blessed him when he laid his right hand upon his head; for although his house was not then so with God as he had hoped, but a house of mourning, a shattered family, yet that promise was sure, as David would one day confess of his own troubled house at 2 Sam. 23:5.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch simply carry the notice forward, joining Ephraim's mourning and his brethren's coming to the birth of the son who would repair the breach. The witnesses do not divide. They take the verse as a rare glimpse of feeling in the midst of a bare register — an old man weeping for his slaughtered children, and kinsmen gathering to comfort him — and they let it stand as proof that behind the dry lists of the Chronicler lay real households, real losses, and a promise that grief could not cancel. The comfort of the brethren, and the covenant that outlived the sorrow, prepare the way for the mercy the next verse records.
23:
Out of mourning comes a birth, and the witnesses read the naming of Beriah as grief turned into a lasting memorial. The reformed John Gill marks the tenderness of the moment: it was after his sorrow had in part subsided that Ephraim went in to his wife, and she conceived and bore a son. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the name and its play. Beriah, he notes, is drawn from a root meaning "gift," yet the text explains it "because in evil it happened in his house" — an allusive play on the words Beriah and bera'ah, such as often meets us in the older Scriptures, at the naming of Noah and at Babel (Gen. 5:29; Gen. 11:9). To call such plays derivations, Ellicott cautions, would be a tasteless anachronism; their purpose is to point a moral, not to teach etymology.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley draws the pastoral sense out fully. By the addition of another son in his old age the breach was in some measure repaired; and when God thus restores comfort to his mourners, making glad according to the days wherein he afflicted, and setting the mercies over against the crosses, we ought to observe the kindness of his providence — the very comfort the psalmist begged at Ps. 90:15. Yet the joy that a son was born could not make the father forget his grief, for he gave the child a melancholy name, Beriah, "in trouble," because he was born while the family was still in mourning. It is good, Wesley adds, to have in remembrance the affliction that is past, that our souls may be humbled within us.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the birth as another Seth given in the place of a slain Abel (Gen. 4:25), a fresh seed raised up after loss. The witnesses do not divide; they gather around one truth, that God comforts the mourner without erasing the memory of his sorrow. Beriah's very name carries the wound even as his birth heals it, so that the household of Ephraim, stricken by the men of Gath, is set on its feet again by the God who gives and restores, and who writes his mercy even into the names of the children of the afflicted.
24:
The register pauses to honour a woman, Sherah, who built cities, and the witnesses delight in the rarity of it. The reformed John Gill takes her to be a descendant of Beriah rather than his immediate daughter, since only so could she have reached the times of Joshua, as the building of her cities implies. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke glosses her name as "remnant," and reports the Targum's reason for it, that she was the remnant which escaped the slaughter the men of Gath had made of Ephraim's sons.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets her work in its geography. As Ephraim's daughter she "built" — which may mean rebuilt, restored, or fortified, as the word is used at Josh. 6:26, Ps. 102:16, and 2 Chron. 11:6 — the two Beth-horons, the nether and the upper, an old Canaanite foundation (Josh. 10:10) still known as the two Beit-urs; and Uzzen-sherah, "Sherah's ear" or peak, a place named after herself and mentioned nowhere else. Her relation to Beth-horon, Ellicott suggests, may be compared with that of Achsah to the Negeb of Judah at Josh. 15:19. Keil and Delitzsch add that Sherah was probably an heiress who had received these places as her inheritance and enlarged and fortified them through her family.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws the moral the witnesses share. It was a further honour to the house of Ephraim that a daughter of the tribe should build cities, one of them bearing her own name; a virtuous woman, he says, may be as great an honour and blessing to a family as a mighty man. The witnesses thus make of this parenthesis a small celebration. Where the slaughter of the sons had broken the house, a daughter now rebuilds its towns and leaves her name upon the land; the God who repaired the breach with the birth of Beriah repairs it further through the works of Sherah, so that the tribe stricken in the fields of Gath is seen rising again in the walls of Beth-horon. Clarke's gloss on her name as the escaped remnant, and Keil and Delitzsch's picture of the heiress fortifying her inherited towns, together let the reader see a providence at work in a woman's building: the daughter whose very name recalls the disaster becomes the restorer of the tribe's strongholds. It is a rare honour in these registers for a woman to be named at all, rarer still for her works to be recorded; and the witnesses take Sherah's cities as proof that the historian would preserve every token of the tribe's recovery, whether it came by the birth of a son or the labour of a daughter.
25:
The line of Beriah is carried down toward its most famous son, and the witnesses trace the descent link by link. The reformed John Gill runs the genealogy from Rephah the son of Beriah through Resheph, Telah, Tahan, Laadan, Ammihud, and Elishama, who was prince of the tribe of Ephraim in the wilderness, to Non, or Nun, whose son was Jehoshua, that is Joshua. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch follow the same chain, marking that Elishama the son of Ammihud was a contemporary of Moses and prince of the tribe of Ephraim, named in the wilderness registers at Num. 1:10 and Num. 7:48; and they note the small oddity that the father of Joshua is here pronounced Non, whereas in the law and the book of Joshua he is Nun.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds his textual eye. The Hebrew, he observes, has "Rephah his son," and the word "his son" may have fallen out after Resheph, so that either Resheph is Rephah's brother and Telah his son, or the copyist has dropped a link. Resheph, meaning "arrow," "lightning," or "fever," was a title of the Phoenician Baal; and Tahan, further down the line, is named as a son of Ephraim and head of a clan at Num. 26:35.

The witnesses feel the quickening interest of the descent. This is no collateral branch counted once and set aside, but the very line that runs to Joshua, the conqueror of Canaan; and so the commentators watch each name with care, fixing Elishama in the Mosaic camp, weighing the dropped "his son," noting the odd form Non. The genealogy that began in the sorrow of Ephraim's slaughtered sons now presses forward, generation by generation, toward the man who would lead Israel into the land — as though the historian, having shown the breach and its repair, means to show also the glory that at last came out of the house of Ephraim, and to root the leader of the conquest firmly in the tribe that had once mourned its children dead in the fields of Gath. Ellicott's textual caution about the dropped "his son," and Keil and Delitzsch's fixing of Elishama in the wilderness camp, are not idle scholarship but the care due to a line that carries such weight; every uncertain link is weighed precisely because the chain ends in Joshua. Gill's plain running of the names from Rephah to Jehoshua keeps the goal always in view, so that the reader follows the descent as a road bending steadily toward the man who would lead Israel over Jordan.
26:
A single generation of the Ephraimite line stands in this verse — Laadan begetting Ammihud, and Ammihud Elishama — and the witnesses fasten on Elishama as the first name in the descent to step out of obscurity into the known history of Israel. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott identifies him plainly: Elishama the son of Ammihud was the tribal prince, the emir of Ephraim, in the time of Moses, named among the heads of the tribes at Num. 7:47.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch had already set him in the same place, marking Elishama the son of Ammihud a contemporary of Moses and prince of the tribe of Ephraim, whose name stands in the wilderness musters and who led the tribe's host on the march (Num. 10:22). The reformed John Gill, tracing the line down to Joshua, likewise pauses at Elishama to call him prince of the tribe of Ephraim in the wilderness, the man who bore the standard of the tribe in the camp of Israel.

The witnesses thus make this bare pair of generations a moment of arrival. For chapters together the register has run through names that appear nowhere else, clans and chieftains known only from these lists; but here, in Elishama, the genealogy touches a figure whose place in the great story is fixed — a prince who stood with Moses, offered at the dedication of the altar, and marshalled the men of Ephraim on their journeys. The commentators do little more than name him, for his history is written elsewhere; yet by naming him they show what the whole descent was for. The line of Ephraim, broken by grief and mended by mercy, is being carried through real and rememberable men toward the conquest, and Elishama, grandfather in the flesh of the coming leader, is the first sure footing on that road, the tribe's prince in the wilderness standing but one generation from Nun and two from Joshua himself. Ellicott's naming of him as the emir of Ephraim, Keil and Delitzsch's placing of him among the standard-bearers on the march, and Gill's simple title of prince of the tribe, all fix the same point: that at Elishama the anonymous stretch of the genealogy gives way to a figure of the Mosaic age whose office and station are known. From here the descent to Joshua is short and certain, and the reader, who has watched clan after nameless clan pass by, feels the register drawing near at last to the great name it has been travelling toward, the promise to Ephraim quietly ripening in the house of Ammihud.
27:
The genealogy reaches its goal, and the witnesses greet the name with evident weight: Non his son, Jehoshua his son. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch bring the descent to rest in Joshua and pass on to the tribe's possessions, noting again that Non is pronounced so only in this place, elsewhere Nun. The reformed John Gill had likewise ended his tracing of the line here, in Non or Nun, whose son was Jehoshua, that is Joshua, the servant and successor of Moses who brought Israel into the land.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott lingers over the descent and what it teaches about reading such lists. Non, he says, is everywhere else Nun, the father of Joshua; and the twenty-fifth to the twenty-seventh verses trace his ancestry through some seven or eight generations up to Rephah the son of Beriah. Yet at the sixth chapter only two names are given between Levi, Ephraim's uncle, and Moses, Joshua's elder contemporary (1 Chron. 6:1). It is obvious to common sense, Ellicott argues, that when it is said Moses was son of Amram, son of Kohath, son of Levi, the meaning cannot be that only two generations lay between the patriarch and Moses; and so these genealogies are not to be read in a slavishly literal spirit, against their own contrary indications. He suspects, too, that Telah in the earlier verse may be a fragment of Shuthelah, and that the true reading once ran "Rephah his son, Shuthelah his son, Tahan his son."

The witnesses close the line of Ephraim on a note of triumph tempered by candour. The tribe that opened in slaughter and mourning ends in Joshua, the captain of the conquest — a fitting crown to the house that Sherah rebuilt and Beriah renewed. Yet Ellicott will not let the reader mistake the compressed register for a literal count of persons, insisting that its very unevenness, seven generations here against two elsewhere, teaches how these lists work: not as an unbroken chain of fathers and sons, but as a faithful record of descent through which the Chronicler brings his people, by whatever number of steps, down to the man in whom the tribe's promise was fulfilled.
28:
The account of Ephraim turns from persons to places, numbering the towns the tribe possessed, and the witnesses read the list as a map of the settled inheritance. The reformed John Gill notes simply that these were the possessions and habitations of the sons of Ephraim once they were come into the land of Canaan — the register passing from the line that led to Joshua to the territory that Joshua's conquest secured.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch supply the geography in detail. The chief places are named on the four sides of the Ephraimite domain: Bethel, now Beitin, with its dependent villages; eastward Naaran; westward Gezer with its towns; and Shechem and its towns, reaching on to Gaza and hers. Bethel, they observe, stood on the frontier between Benjamin and Ephraim (Josh. 16:2) and was assigned to Benjamin (Josh. 18:22); yet here it is reckoned an Ephraimite city on the southern border, because it belonged to the kingdom of the ten tribes — a plain token, they judge, that this register was drawn up after that northern kingdom had come into being. The "daughters" of each town are the smaller villages that clustered about it.

The witnesses find in the list more than a gazetteer. That Bethel should be counted to Ephraim rather than Benjamin lets Keil and Delitzsch date the whole record to the days of the divided monarchy, when the old tribal borders had shifted under the new kingdom; the historian, copying his source, preserves the boundaries as they stood in his own later age. And the sweep of the names, from Bethel in the south to Shechem and Gezer, marks out the heartland that the sons of Joseph held in the centre of the land. Gill's brief notice and Keil and Delitzsch's careful topography meet in one point: the tribe whose line ran down to Joshua was given a broad and settled home in Canaan, and the Chronicler sets its towns on record that the reader might see the promise to Ephraim made good in the very soil the tribe possessed. That Keil and Delitzsch should read the very counting of Bethel to Ephraim as a mark of the register's late date only deepens the point, for the historian preserves the map of the tribe as it stood in his own age, the borders shifted by the rise of the northern kingdom; and the "daughters" that cluster about each named town remind the reader that behind every great city lay a ring of villages, so that the possession of Ephraim was no scattering of outposts but a thickly settled land.
29:
The possessions of Joseph's sons are completed with the border-towns of Manasseh, and the witnesses read the closing notice as the seal on the whole Joseph inheritance. The reformed John Gill marks that these places lay by the borders of the children of Manasseh, near which the Ephraimites dwelt, so that the two tribes of Joseph held the land side by side. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch name the four cities — Bethshean, Taanach, Megiddo, and Dor, each with its dependent towns — and explain that, according to Josh. 17:11, the Manassites had received these cities though they lay within the territories of Issachar and Asher. The phrase that they stood "in the hands" of the sons of Manasseh attests their possession; and these towns, they add, formed the boundary on the extreme north of the dwellings of the sons of Joseph.

The closing words of the verse draw the witnesses' notice most of all: "in these dwelt the children of Joseph the son of Israel." Here the historian gathers Ephraim and Manasseh together under their common father, and sets the whole of their territory, from Bethel in the south to Bethshean and Dor in the north, under the single title of Joseph's inheritance. The two tribes that sprang from the son sold into Egypt are seen at last planted broad across the centre and north of the land.

The witnesses do not divide, and the note carries a quiet fitness. Joseph, once torn from his father and mourned as dead, is here the father of a people filling four great cities and the hill-country besides; the boy whom his brethren cast out has become, in his sons, a settled multitude in the promised land. Keil and Delitzsch's care to show how the Manassites held towns within their neighbours' borders, and Gill's simple placing of Ephraim beside Manasseh, together let the reader see the breadth of the blessing that had come upon Joseph's house. Keil and Delitzsch's care to show that Bethshean and Megiddo, Taanach and Dor lay within the lands of Issachar and Asher yet were held by Manasseh, and Gill's simple placing of Ephraim beside its brother-tribe, together mark how the house of Joseph overspread its neighbours, reaching cities beyond its own allotted borders. With this the historian closes the record of the central tribes and turns north to the last of his musters, the sons of Asher.
30:
The register turns to Asher, and the witnesses open the account by setting his children beside the older records. The reformed John Gill names the sons — Imnah, Isuah, Ishuai, and Beriah — with Serah their sister, noting that Asher and his grandsons born before Jacob went down to Egypt are reckoned here as in the law, only that Malchiel his second grandson is now added. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch confirm that the four sons and the sister coincide with the statement of Gen. 46:17, though in Num. 26:44 the name Ishuai does not appear among the families of Asher; and they carry the line on to the sons of Beriah, Heber and Malchiel.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, surveying the tribe at the head of its account, marks the character of Asher's greatness. Some men of note are here named, and though the tribe's militia was not numerous in comparison with others — only twenty-six thousand men in all — their princes were choice and mighty men of valour, chief of the princes. Perhaps, Henry suggests, it was their wisdom that they coveted not to make their trained bands many, but rather to have a few, and those apt for the war and serviceable men.

The witnesses thus give Asher a double frame. Gill and Keil and Delitzsch anchor the tribe in the ancient registers, matching son for son with Genesis and marking where the law and the Chronicler differ, so that Asher takes his place among the twelve exactly as descent required. Henry lifts his eyes to the quality of the tribe, reading in its modest numbers and its excellent princes a lesson that strength lies not in multitude but in men fit for their work. And the sister Serah, kept here as in the law, reminds the reader that Asher's house, like Ephraim's and Manasseh's, preserved even its daughters in the sacred roll. The account of the last tribe begins, then, in fidelity to the old records and in praise of a people few but valiant, whose choice princes the following verses will name. Keil and Delitzsch's note that Ishuai is missing from the families of Asher in the law, while present here and in Genesis, is one more instance of the small differences the historian preserves without smoothing, and Gill's marking of Malchiel as an addition beyond the older lists shows the Chronicler filling out the record where his sources allowed. The tribe that Deborah once reproached for lingering by its harbours is here honoured at the head of its genealogy for the excellence of its chiefs, a reminder that the worth of a people is not always to be read from its numbers.
31:
The line of Beriah, Asher's youngest son, is carried on, and the witnesses attend to its two heads and to a name found nowhere else. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that the sons of Beriah, Heber and Malchiel, stand also in Gen. 46:17 and Num. 26:45 as heads of two families of Asher; but the further clause, that Malchiel was "the father of Birzavith," is found only here. How the word is to be pronounced, they own, cannot be certainly determined: Gesenius took it for the name of a woman, while Bertheau conjectured it a compound meaning "well of the olive-tree," and so the name of a place. The reformed John Gill, for his part, had marked Malchiel as Asher's second grandson, added in this fuller register beyond what the earlier lists preserved.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry keeps before the reader the worth of the tribe whose branches these are. Asher's men of note, though the tribe was small, were choice and mighty men of valour, chief of the princes; and it was perhaps their wisdom, he thinks, to prize quality over numbers, seeking a few serviceable men rather than a great host.

The witnesses leave the obscure Birzavith unforced, content to weigh whether it names a woman or a well without pressing a decision the text does not yield. Yet even here they find the Chronicler's fidelity at work: he sets down what his source preserved, an extra clause about Malchiel's line that the older registers had let fall, careless neither of the added name nor of its uncertainty. Keil and Delitzsch's sober "cannot be determined" is itself a kind of faithfulness, refusing to invent a meaning where the record is dark. So the house of Beriah is traced one step further into Asher's genealogy, Heber and Malchiel standing as the founders of two families, and the strange Birzavith kept in its place as a witness that the historian copied even what he could not fully explain, that nothing of the tribe's descent might be lost. Whether the word name a woman married into the house, as Gesenius thought, or a well among the olive-groves where the family dwelt, as Bertheau supposed, the witnesses will not decide beyond the evidence; and their restraint is instructive, for these registers are strewn with such half-legible names, and the reader learns from Keil and Delitzsch to receive them as they stand rather than to press a meaning the text withholds. The clause about Malchiel, added here alone, is one more grain the Chronicler gathered from a fuller source, kept lest even the least of Asher's houses should drop from the record.
32:
The line of Heber, Asher's elder grandson, is opened, and the witnesses name his children and pause over a sister. The reformed John Gill marks Heber as the grandson of Asher, son of Beriah. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set out his household: Heber begat Japhlet, Shomer, and Hotham, with Shua their sister; and they run ahead to the branches that follow, the sons of Japhlet and of Shomer, and the sons of a certain Helem, whom they suspect to be the same as this Hotham under a name that a copyist has altered, since one of the two forms must have arisen from an error in transcription.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fastens on the sister. It is very rarely, he observes, that women are found in the Jewish genealogies, and they are never inserted but for some especial reason. That Shua is named at all, therefore, marks her out as a woman of consequence in the tribe, though the reason for her remembrance is now hidden from us.

The witnesses read the verse as the branching of Asher's chief house. Where the tribe's princes were, as Henry said, choice and mighty men, here their descent is set down in order, Heber begetting the fathers of several clans; and the naming of Shua among her brothers gives the register a touch of that same honour it paid to Serah the sister of Asher and to Sherah who built the cities of Ephraim. Clarke's rule, that women enter these lists only for a cause, invites the reader to reckon Shua no idle addition but a person of standing whose memory the tribe kept. And Keil and Delitzsch's suspicion that Hotham and Helem are one man under two spellings prepares the reader for the difficulty of the verses ahead, where the sons of Asher multiply into clans and the same names shift their forms. The house of Heber, at any rate, is now set on record, its three sons and their sister the stems from which the following musters of Asher will grow. Clarke's rule that a woman enters the pedigree only for cause, and Keil and Delitzsch's early suspicion that Hotham and Helem are one man under two spellings, together set the reader on guard for the verses to come, where the sons of Japhlet, Shomer, and Helem branch into many clans and the same names shift and recur. The witnesses, by naming Shua and weighing the doubtful Hotham at the outset, teach that even the head of this genealogy must be read with care, each name and each sister marking something the tribe thought worth remembering.
33:
The sons of Japhlet are named — Pasach, Bimhal, and Ashvath — and the witnesses can do little more than record them, for the men appear in no other place. The reformed John Gill says as much plainly: these are the children of Japhlet, of whom we read not elsewhere. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, gathering the descendants of Heber, set down the same three as the sons of Japhlet, the closing words marking that the list of this branch is complete — "these are the children of Japhlet" — before the register turns to Japhlet's brother Shomer.

The witnesses' very brevity is instructive. There is no story to tell of Pasach or Bimhal or Ashvath, no city named after them, no deed remembered; and the commentators, faithful to their sources, do not invent one. They name the men and pass on, as the Chronicler himself does, content that the branch of Japhlet should have its place in the muster of Asher even though nothing survives of its history beyond the bare descent.

Yet in that restraint the witnesses find the temper of the whole genealogy. The Chronicler is not writing the annals of Asher but preserving its pedigree, the roll of the fathers-houses through whom the tribe's fighting men would presently be numbered; and a household counted once and never heard of again is kept for the same reason as the famous names — that the tribe's descent might stand whole. The sons of Japhlet are three such quiet entries, links in the chain that binds Asher to the twelve, remembered by name in the sacred record though the world forgot them. The witnesses honour that fidelity, refusing to pad the silence, and let the little branch stand as it is: three sons of a son of Heber, named that they might not be lost, and left in the peace of their obscurity while the register moves on to Shomer's line. Gill's blunt "of whom we read not elsewhere" and Keil and Delitzsch's bare enumeration are, in their way, a model of how such lists ask to be read — with acknowledgment rather than embellishment. The closing formula, "these are the children of Japhlet," rounds off the branch as neatly as a longer house, and the reader is reminded that in the sacred pedigree the least clan is granted the same dignity of a full and finished entry as the greatest, so that Asher's descent might be traced without a gap from son to son and house to house.
34:
The sons of Shomer are given, and the witnesses work to fix the man's name and to read the foreign strain in his household. The reformed John Gill names him Shamer, or Shomer, the brother of Japhlet. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott takes Shamer for a pausal form of Shemer, probably identical with the Shomer who stood as Heber's second son in the previous verses; and he weighs the several names of this line. The Hebrew margin, he notes, reads "and Hubbah" for Jehubbah, which is the more correct according to the prevailing form of the list, where each name is joined by "and"; and Aram, the ordinary name of the Syrians east and west of the Euphrates, may here mark a clan of half-Aramean extraction, a foreign strand woven into Asher as into Manasseh before it.

Ellicott looks ahead to the next branch as well, "the sons of his brother Helem," and finds a difficulty. If "brother" be taken strictly, Helem must be the same as the Hotham of Heber's sons, one of the two names being corrupt; but Helem may rather be the name of another chief house of Asher not directly sprung from Heber, in which case the brotherhood would be that of the tribe and not of the immediate family. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch simply enumerate the sons of Shamer — Ahi, Rohgah, Jehubbah, and Aram — as they carry the descent of Heber through its generations.

The witnesses thus turn a short list into a study of the tribe's making. The variant "Hubbah" for Jehubbah shows the small shifts of spelling that run through these registers; the name Aram hints that Asher, like its neighbours, had drawn Syrian blood into its clans; and the puzzle of Helem's brotherhood warns again that a word like "brother" may mean kinsman of the tribe as easily as son of the same father. Ellicott's patience with each name, and Keil and Delitzsch's steady enumeration, leave the house of Shomer set firmly in the roll, its sons named and its uncertainties honestly marked, so that the descent of Heber may be followed without either forcing the difficulties or passing them by.
35:
The register turns to the sons of Helem, and the witnesses take up in earnest the question his name raises. The reformed John Gill states it at once: these are the sons of Helem his brother, that is the brother of Shomer, and Helem, he judges on the authority of Hillerus, is the same man as the Hotham named among Heber's sons. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch had reasoned to the same end, taking Helem for the third son of Heber, elsewhere called Hotham, so that one of the two names must have arisen from an error in transcription, though which is the original form it is now impossible to determine.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, however, keeps the alternative open.
