If "brother" be understood strictly, then indeed Helem is Hotham under a corrupted name; but Helem may equally be the head of another chief house of Asher, not directly connected with Heber's line, and then the brotherhood would be that of the tribe rather than of the family. The sons here named — Zophah, Imna, Shelesh, and Amal — begin a large branch that the following verses will multiply into many clans.

So a real if minor debate stands over the verse. Gill and Keil and Delitzsch would identify Helem with Hotham and lay the difference to a scribe's slip; Ellicott would allow that Helem may be a distinct chieftain, the "brother" being tribal, and so no error at all. The witnesses do not force the point, for the text yields no certainty; and their very disagreement illustrates the caution these registers demand, where a single name may be a variant spelling of one already met or the head of a wholly separate house. What none of them doubts is that Helem's sons belong to the muster of Asher, a branch of the tribe whose fighting men the chapter is bent on counting; and whether Helem be Hotham renewed or a fresh name, his line is set down that the strength of Asher, gathered clan by clan, might stand complete in the sacred record. The difference between Gill and Keil and Delitzsch on the one side, laying the matter to a scribe's slip, and Ellicott on the other, allowing a distinct chieftain and a merely tribal brotherhood, is small in itself, yet it opens the largest branch of the whole tribe, for from Helem's sons will spring Zophah's eleven and all the houses that follow. The witnesses handle it with the same care they have given every knot in the chapter, weighing a name rather than forcing it, so that the great muster of Asher may rest on a descent honestly traced.
36:
The house of Zophah, eldest of Helem's sons, opens into a broad list, and the witnesses number its sons and mark one name as strange. The reformed John Gill notes simply that Zophah was the eldest of the sons of Helem. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set down his sons — Suah, Harnepher, Shual, Beri, and Imrah — and count the whole branch of Zophah at eleven names across this verse and the next, before the descent passes to Jether and to Ulla.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott dwells on the second of the sons. Harnepher, he observes, bears a name that looks like pure Egyptian, "Har nefer," the beauteous Horus, or the morning sun; and he sets it beside other traces of Egypt in these registers — the Egyptian slave Jarha, and the marriage of Mered with a daughter of Pharaoh at 1 Chron. 4:18. Zophah, he adds, is the elder house among the branches of Helem, from which this longest stretch of the Asherite genealogy springs.

The witnesses read the verse as the flowering of the tribe into its many clans. Where the earlier branches yielded three sons or four, here eleven are gathered under Zophah, a great house of Asher spreading into households whose names, for the most part, are met nowhere else. Ellicott's eye for the Egyptian cast of Harnepher opens a small window on the tribe's mixed inheritance, a name from the land of the Nile lodged among the sons of Jacob's Asher, as elsewhere a Syrian Aram had been. The commentators do not press the point beyond the naming, for the men have no recorded history; but they let the strangeness stand as one more sign that Israel's tribes had drawn into themselves the peoples among whom their fathers had lived. The house of Zophah, at any rate, is now set on record, its eleven sons the largest single branch of Asher, and the muster of the tribe swells toward the total the chapter will presently give. Ellicott's comparison of Harnepher with the Egyptian slave Jarha and with Mered's marriage to a daughter of Pharaoh sets this Asherite name within a wider pattern he has traced through the Chronicles, of Egyptian blood and Egyptian names lodged among the tribes of Israel; and Keil and Delitzsch's plain listing of Suah and Shual and the rest, with no story to tell of any, shows how the genealogy has passed into its most purely documentary stretch. The eleven sons of Zophah are heads of fathers-houses and nothing more is known of them, yet each is kept, for each was a household whose men would swell the twenty-six thousand of the tribe's array.
37:
The count of Zophah's house runs on into a second rank of names — Bezer, Hod, Shamma, Shilshah, Ithran, and Beera — and the witnesses gather them into the eleven that made up this largest branch of Asher. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, treating the whole roll of Zophah together, enumerate these sons as the continuation of the list begun in the verse before, and reckon eleven names in all as the sons of Zophah, before the register turns to the three sons of Jether and the descendants of Ulla.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, who called Zophah the elder house among the branches of Helem, likewise counts the eleven names of his sons as the fullest stretch of the Asherite genealogy, a single household spreading into a great company of clans. Neither witness finds a story to attach to Bezer or Hod or the rest, for the names appear in no other passage of Scripture; they are the heads of fathers-houses within Asher, known only from this register.

The witnesses' quiet enumeration is itself the point. Here, near the end of the chapter, the genealogy is at its most sheerly documentary — name after name with neither deed nor place recorded — and the commentators, faithful to their sources, do no more than count them and set them in their branch. Yet the very fullness of Zophah's list, eleven sons where other houses had three or four, shows why the historian took such pains: these were the fathers-houses whose grown men would swell the tribe's muster, and each name kept is a household preserved in the roll of Asher. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott let the bare names stand, refusing to embroider what the text leaves plain, and so honour the Chronicler's own restraint. The sons of Zophah, gathered into their eleven, close the great central branch of the tribe, and the register moves on to Jether and Ulla to complete the descent before the final reckoning of Asher's warriors. That the source-catena preserves here almost nothing but the bare names — the fuller comments of Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott reaching over this verse from their notes on the branch as a whole — only sharpens the impression of a genealogy laid down for the record's sake, name after name entrusted to the sacred page though no deed cleaves to them. The witnesses meet that silence with silence of their own, adding no legend and inventing no history, and so let Bezer and Hod and Beera keep the quiet place the Chronicler gave them among the fathers-houses of Asher.
38:
A short branch follows, the sons of Jether, and the witnesses connect it back to the house of Zophah and note a famous name among the fathers. The reformed John Gill takes Jether to be the same as Ithran, the last but one of Zophah's sons in the verse before. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott agrees that Jether and Ithran are virtually the same name and probably to be identified here, which links this verse with the preceding line of the sons of Zophah; and he marks that Jephunneh, the first of Jether's three sons, bears the name of the father of Caleb the Kenizzite, the faithful spy who with Joshua brought back a good report of the land (Num. 13:6) — though the Asherite Jephunneh is of course another man. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, gathering the branches of Zophah's house, likewise set down the three sons of Jether — Jephunneh, Pispah, and Ara.

The witnesses read the verse as one more knitting-together of the tribe's clans. If Jether be Ithran, then this little household is no fresh stem but a continuation of Zophah's great branch, the register looping back to carry a son's line forward; and the recurrence of the name Jephunneh, borne elsewhere by the father of Caleb, reminds the reader how the same names ran through many tribes and households of Israel, so that a name honoured in Judah sounds again here in Asher without marking the same family.

The commentators do not labour the point beyond these identifications, for the sons of Jether, like so many in this chapter, have no history but their descent. Yet the witnesses find in the verse the same careful fidelity they have marked throughout: the Chronicler, or his source, preserves even the smallest branch, ties it by the name Ithran to the house from which it sprang, and sets down its three sons that the muster of Asher might be complete. Ellicott's linking of Jether to Zophah, and his note on the borrowed name Jephunneh, let the reader see how tightly these lists are woven, one household into another, down to the last reckoning of the tribe. Gill's quiet identification of Jether with Ithran, and Keil and Delitzsch's gathering of the three sons under the branch of Zophah, converge with Ellicott to keep the descent unbroken, so that no household of Asher is left to hang loose from the tree. It is the same labour the commentators have performed at every turn of the chapter, joining a stray name to its stem; and here, near the close, they perform it once more, that the pedigree of the tribe might run whole and continuous from the sons of Beriah to the very last of the fathers-houses.
39:
The last branch of Asher is named, the sons of Ulla, and the witnesses close the descent by trying to fit this final household into the line before it. The reformed John Gill supposes Ulla to be either the son of the Ara last mentioned or else another son of Jether. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reasons more closely: Ulla seems at first not connected with the foregoing genealogy at all, yet he takes him to be the same as the Ara of the previous verse. The form "Ara," he judges, is a very curious one, perhaps due to a copyist's eye having wandered to the Be-era at the end of an earlier verse, while "Ulla" is intelligible and probably the correct name; and if the identification be allowed, we gain a complete concatenation of the whole line from the thirtieth verse to the thirty-ninth, the branches of Asher joined without a break. He adds the small caution that Arah, elsewhere in the chapter, is in Hebrew quite a different word from this Ara.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, completing the descendants of Zophah's house, set down the sons of Ulla — Arah, Haniel, and Rezia — as the last of the fathers-houses of Asher to be named before the summary.

The witnesses thus bring the genealogy of Asher to a tidy close, and Ellicott's textual care is the making of it. By reading Ulla as Ara restored, he binds the final household to the rest, so that the whole tribe, from the sons of Beriah down to the sons of Ulla, stands in one unbroken descent. It is a fitting end to a chapter that has everywhere shown the historian, and his interpreters after him, labouring to keep the lines whole — mending a dropped "his son," distinguishing a variant spelling, joining a stray branch to its stem. The sons of Ulla, obscure as they are, complete the roll of Asher's fathers-houses; and with their names set down, the register is ready for the final reckoning of the tribe's mighty men, to which the closing verse now turns. Keil and Delitzsch's plain listing of Arah, Haniel, and Rezia, and Ellicott's ingenious repair that reads Ulla as Ara restored, meet in the one aim that has governed the whole chapter — to leave no branch dangling and no name unaccounted. Where Gill was content to guess that Ulla sprang from Ara or from Jether, Ellicott's textual eye binds the household firmly to the line, so that the tribe's descent stands complete from the thirtieth verse to the thirty-ninth, a continuous chain of fathers-houses. It is a fitting last labour of the commentators before the muster itself is given, this careful joining of the final clan to its stem, that the strength of Asher might rest upon a pedigree traced without a break.
40:
The muster of Asher is summed, and with it the whole chapter closes, and the witnesses read the final verse as both a tribute and a sober reckoning. The reformed John Gill takes the summary at its plain sense: all these were the children of Asher, heads of their fathers' house, principal men in their tribe and their families. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke adds a touch from Jewish tradition, that the daughters of Asher were reputed very beautiful and were all matched with kings or with priests, and refers the reader to the parallel places for the rest.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders and weighs the closing words. All these were picked chiefs of the fathers-houses, valiant warriors, chiefs of the princes; and their number, mustered for the host and the battle, was twenty-six thousand men — a census, he stresses, that has reference only to the males qualified for military service. He sets it against the older reckonings: in the Mosaic census the tribe stood at forty-one thousand five hundred (Num. 1:41), and a generation later at fifty-three thousand fighting men (Num. 26:47). The date of the present census is not given, but if it be David's, the tribe may by then have declined in numbers and importance — a decline in keeping with the old reproach of Deborah, that "Asher continued at the sea-shore, and abode on his creeks," and did not bestir himself for the war (Judg. 5:17).

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns the smallness into praise: though the militia of Asher was but twenty-six thousand, their princes were choice and mighty men of valour, and perhaps it was their wisdom to prize a few serviceable men above a great host. So the witnesses close the chapter on a mingled note. Asher's numbers had fallen since the days of Moses, and Deborah's rebuke still hung over the tribe that loved its harbours more than the battle; yet its chiefs were valiant and its daughters honoured, and its fighting men are counted here, as the men of every tribe have been, that the strength of Israel, gathered from Issachar to Asher, might stand whole in the sacred register.
